concepts.title: Microscope
concepts.conceptBody:
Microscope

It is an optical instrument used to increase the visual angle of near objects which are too small to be seen by naked eye.

Construction 

It consists of two convergent lenses of short focal lengths and apertures arranged co-axially. Lens (of focal length ${{\text{f}}_{0}}$ ) facing the object is called objective or field lens while the lens (of focal length ${{\text{f}}_{\text{e}}}$ ) facing the eye, eye-piece or ocular. The objective has a smaller aperture and smaller focal length than eye-piece. The separation between objective and eye-piece can be varied.

Image Formation 

 

The object is placed between $\text{F}$ and $2\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ F}$ of objective, so the image ${{\text{I}}_{\text{M}}}$ formed by objective (called intermediate image) is inverted, real, enlarged and a distance greater than $2{{\text{f}}_{0}}$ on the other side of the lens. This image ${{I}_{M}}$ acts as object for eye-piece and is within its focus. So eye-piece forms final image I which is erect, virtual and enlarged with respect to intermediate image ${{\text{I}}_{\text{M}}}$. So the final image $\text{I}$ with respect to object is inverted, virtual enlarged and at a distance $\text{D}$ to from eye on the same side of eye-piece as ${{\text{I}}_{\text{M}}}$.

concepts.title: Magnifying Power of microscope
concepts.conceptBody:
Magnifying Power

Magnifying power of an optical instrument is defined as :

$\text{MP}=\frac{ \text{ Visual } \text{ angle } \text{ with } \text{ instrument } }{ \text{ Max}\text{. } \text{ visual } \text{ angle } \text{ for } \text{ unaided } \text{ eye } }=\frac{\theta }{{{\theta }_{0}}}$

If the size of object is $\text{h}$ and least distance of distinct vision is $\text{D}$

${{\theta }_{0}}=\frac{\text{h}}{\text{D}} \text{ and } \theta =\frac{{\text{{h}'}}}{{{\text{u}}_{\text{e}}}}$

So $MP=\left[ \frac{{{h}'}}{{{u}_{e}}} \right]\times \left[ \frac{D}{h} \right]=\left[ \frac{{{h}'}}{h} \right]\left[ \frac{D}{{{u}_{e}}} \right]$

But for objective

$\begin{array}{l} {} &  \text{m}=\frac{\text{I}}{\text{O}}=-\frac{\text{v}}{\text{u}} \\  \text{ i}\text{.e}\text{., }   & \left. \frac{{\text{{h}'}}}{\text{h}}=-\frac{\text{v}}{\text{u}}  [\text{as } \text{ u } \text{ is } - \text{ ive}  \right] \\  \text{ So, }  &  \text{MP}=-\frac{\text{v}}{\text{u}}\left[ \frac{\text{D}}{{{\text{u}}_{\text{e}}}} \right] \\ {} &   \\ \end{array}$

with length of tube $\text{L}=\text{v}+{{\text{u}}_{\text{e}}}$ …(1)

Now there are two possibilities :

If the final image is at infinity (Far point) 

This situation is called normal adjustment as in this situation eye is least strained or relaxed. In this situation as for eye-piece $\text{v}=\infty $,

$\frac{1}{-\infty }-\frac{1}{-{{u}_{e}}}=\frac{1}{{{f}_{e}}} \text{ i}\text{.e}\text{., } {{u}_{e}}={{f}_{e}}= \text{ maximum }$

Substituting this value of ue in Eq. (1), we have

$MP=-\frac{v}{u}\left[ \frac{D}{{{f}_{e}}} \right] \text{ with } L=v+{{f}_{e}}$ …(2)

A microscope is usually considered to operate in this mode unless stated otherwise. In this mode as ${{u}_{e}}$ is maximum, MP is minimum for a given microscope.

If the final image is at $D$ (Near point) 

In this situation as for eye-piece $\text{v}=\text{D}$

$\frac{1}{-D}-\frac{1}{-{{u}_{e}}}=\frac{1}{{{f}_{e}}}\text{, }\!\!~\!\!\text{ i}\text{.e}\text{., }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }\frac{1}{{{u}_{e}}}=\frac{1}{D}\left[ 1+\frac{D}{{{f}_{e}}} \right]$

Substituting this value of ${{u}_{e}}$ in Eq. (1), we have

$MP=-\frac{v}{u}\left[ 1+\frac{D}{{{f}_{e}}} \right]$

With $\text{L}=\text{v}+\frac{{{\text{f}}_{\text{e}}}\text{D}}{\left( {{\text{f}}_{\text{e}}}+\text{D} \right)}$ …(3)

In this situation as ${{u}_{e}}$ is minimum MP is maximum and eye is most strained.

In case of microscope as ${{f}_{0}}$ is small and object is close to objective $u\simeq {{f}_{0}}$. Also as intermediate image is in front of eye lens which has very short focal length, length of tube.

$\text{L}=\text{v}+{{\text{u}}_{\text{e}}}\approx \text{v }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }\left[ \text{as}{{\text{u}}_{\text{e}}}<\text{v} \right]$

So for normal adjustment, i.e., from Eq. (2)

…(4)

concepts.title: Special Cases in Microscope
concepts.conceptBody:
Important Points of Microscope

1.As magnifying power is negative, the image seen in a microscope is always truly inverted, i.e., left is turned right with upside down simultaneously.

2.As intermediate image is between the two lenses, crosswire (a measuring device) can be used. So with microscope measurements can be made along with observations.

3.As $\text{m}=\frac{\text{v}}{-\text{u}}$ and ${{\text{m}}_{\theta }}=\frac{\text{D}}{{{\text{u}}_{\text{e}}}}$, Eq. (1) gives $\text{MP}=\text{m }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }{{\text{m}}_{\theta }}$

i.e., magnifying power of a microscope is equal to the product of longitudinal magnification of objective and angular magnification of eye-piece.

4.If final image is at least distance of distinct vision

$\frac{1}{-D}-\frac{1}{-{{u}_{e}}}=\frac{1}{{{f}_{e}}}\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ i}\text{.e}\text{., }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }{{m}_{e}}=\frac{{{v}_{e}}}{{{u}_{e}}}=\frac{D}{{{u}_{e}}}=\left[ 1+\frac{D}{{{f}_{e}}} \right]={{m}_{\theta }}$

So MP $=\text{m }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }{{\text{m}}_{\theta }}=\text{m }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }{{\text{m}}_{\text{e}}}=$ Linear magnification

5.For a microscope MP is minimum when final image is at $\infty $ and maximum when final image is at least distance of distinct vision D, i.e., ${{(MP)}_{\text{min}}}=-\frac{v}{u}\frac{D}{{{f}_{e}}}$ and ${{(MP)}_{\text{max}}}=-\frac{v}{u}\left[ 1+\frac{D}{{{f}_{e}}} \right]$

6.For a given microscope MP for normal setting remains practically unchanged if field and eye lens are interchanged as $\text{MP}\simeq \left[ \text{LD}/{{\text{f}}_{0}}{{\text{f}}_{\text{e}}} \right]$.

7.As the aperture of both the lenses in a microscope is small, the defects of images particularly spherical aberration is minimum.

8.As MP for normal setting is $\left( LD/{{f}_{0}}{{f}_{e}} \right)$, so to have large MP, ${{f}_{0}}$ and ${{f}_{e}}$ must be as small as practically possible. This is why in a microscope both the lenses have small focal lengths.

9.Out of ${{f}_{0}}$ and ${{f}_{e}},{{f}_{0}}$ is taken to be smaller, so that field of view may be increased and objective being closer to object may collect more light to increase brightness of the image. This also ensures that intermediate image is in front of eye lens within its focus.

10.While working with a microscope the eye must be close to eye-piece, i.e, persons wearing specks should take off their specks (if possible) otherwise MP will be adversely affected. This is because if eye is at a distance $d$ from the eye-piece, the distance of final image from eyepiece will be $\text{{D}'}=\left( \text{D}-\text{d} \right)$ and hence $\text{MP}$ will become

$\text{M}{{\text{P}}^{1}}\frac{\text{L}{{\text{D}}^{\text{ }\!\!'\!\!\text{ }}}}{{{\text{f}}_{0}}{{\text{f}}_{\text{e}}}}=\frac{\text{L}\left( \text{D}-\text{d} \right)}{{{\text{f}}_{0}}{{\text{f}}_{\text{e}}}}=\text{MP}\left[ 1-\frac{\text{d}}{\text{D}} \right]<\text{MP}$

11.In reference to a microscope, the minimum distance between two lines at which they are just distinct is called limit of resolution and reciprocal of limit of resolution is called resolving power which for a microscope varies inversely with wavelength, i.e.,

$\text{R}{{\text{P}}^{\text{*}}}=\frac{1}{\text{ }\!\!\Delta\!\!\text{ x}}\propto \frac{1}{\lambda }$

Smaller the limit of resolution of higher the resolving power, more details of object will be visible in the image. In case of microscope $\text{RP}$ is increased by decreasing the wavelength of light used and we have ultra-microscope and electron-microscope.

Note : In case of electron-microscope it has been shown that wavelength of electron $\lambda =\sqrt{\left( 150/\text{V} \right)}$ where $\text{V}$ is the accelerating voltage; so $\text{RP}$ of electron microscope will be proportional to $\sqrt{\text{V}}$ and can be up to about ${{10}^{5}}$ times that of optical microscope.

concepts.title: Telescope
concepts.conceptBody:
Telescope

It is an optical instrument used to increase the visual angle of distant large objects such as a star, a planet or a cliff, etc.

 

Astronomical telescope consists of two converging lenses. The one facing the object is called objective or field lens and has large focal length and aperture while the other facing the eye called eyepiece or ocular has small focal length and aperture. The distance between the two lenses is adjustable.

As a telescope is used to see distant objects, in it object is between $\infty $ and $2\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ F}$ of objective and hence image formed by objective is real, inverted and diminished and is between $\text{F}$ and $2\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ F}$ on the other side of it. This image (called intermediate image) acts as an object for eye-piece and by shifting the position of eye-piece, it is brought within its focus. So final image I, with respect to intermediate image, is erect, virtual, enlarged and at a distance $\text{D}$ to $\infty $ from the eye. This is turn implies that final image with respect to object is inverted, enlarged and a distance $\text{D}$ to $\infty $ from the eye.

concepts.title: Magnifying Power of telescope
concepts.conceptBody:
Magnifying Power

Magnifying power of a telescope is defined as,

$\text{MP}=\frac{\text{ Visual }\text{ angle }\text{ with }\text{ instrument }}{\text{ Visual }\text{ angle }\text{ for }\text{ unaided }\text{ eye }}=\frac{\theta }{{{\theta }_{0}}}$

But from Fig.

${{\theta }_{0}}=\frac{\text{y}}{{{\text{f}}_{0}}}\text{ and }\theta =\frac{\text{y}}{-{{\text{u}}_{\text{e}}}}$

So, $\text{MP}=\frac{\theta }{{{\theta }_{0}}}=-\left[ \frac{{{\text{f}}_{0}}}{{{\text{u}}_{\text{e}}}} \right]$

with length of tube $\text{L}=\left( {{\text{f}}_{0}}+{{\text{u}}_{\text{e}}} \right)$ …(1)

Now there are two possibilities :

1.If the final image is at infinity (Far point) 

This situation is called normal adjustment as in this situation eye is least strained or relaxed. In this situation as for eye-piece $\text{v}=\infty $,

$\frac{1}{-\infty }-\frac{1}{-{{u}_{e}}}=\frac{1}{{{f}_{e}}}\text{ i}\text{.e}\text{., }{{u}_{e}}={{f}_{e}}$

So substituting this value of ${{u}_{e}}$ in Eq. (1), we have

$MP=-\left( {{f}_{0}}/{{f}_{e}} \right)\text{ and }L=\left( {{f}_{0}}+{{f}_{e}} \right)$

Usually, a telescope operates in this mode unless stated otherwise. In this mode as ${{u}_{\text{e}}}$ is maximum, for a given telescope MP is minimum while length of tube maximum.

2.If the final image is at $D$ (Near point) 

In this situation as for eye-piece, $\text{v}=\text{D}$

$\frac{1}{-D}-\frac{1}{-{{u}_{e}}}=\frac{1}{{{f}_{e}}}\text{ i}\text{.e}\text{., }\frac{1}{{{u}_{e}}}=\frac{1}{{{f}_{e}}}\left[ 1+\frac{{{f}_{e}}}{D} \right]$

So substituting this value of ue in Eq. (1), we have

$MP=-\frac{{{f}_{0}}}{{{f}_{e}}}\left[ 1+\frac{{{f}_{e}}}{D} \right]$

with $\text{ L}={{\text{f}}_{0}}+\frac{{{\text{f}}_{\text{e}}}\text{D}}{\left( {{\text{f}}_{\text{e}}}+\text{D} \right)}$

In this situation ${{u}_{e}}$ is minimum, so for a given telescope MP is maximum while length of tube minimum and eye is most strained.

In case of a telescope, if object and final image are at infinity and total light entering the telescope leaves it parallel to its axis as shown in Fig.,

$\frac{{{\text{f}}_{0}}}{{{\text{f}}_{\text{e}}}}=\frac{\text{ Aperture }\text{ of }\text{ objective }}{\text{ Aperture }\text{ of }\text{ eye-piece }}$

i.e., $MP=\frac{{{f}_{0}}}{{{f}_{e}}}=\frac{D}{d}$

concepts.title: Special Cases in Telescope
concepts.conceptBody:
Special Cases in Telescope

1.As magnifying power is negative, the image seen in astronomical telescope is truly inverted, i.e., left is turned right with upside down simultaneously. However, as most of the astronomical objects are symmetrical this inversion does not affect the observations.

2.For given telescope, magnifying power is minimum when final image is at infinity (Far point) and maximum when it is at distance of distinct vision (Near point), i.e.,

${{(MP)}_{\text{min}}}=-\left[ \frac{{{f}_{0}}}{{{f}_{e}}} \right]\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ and }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }{{(MP)}_{\text{max}}}=-\frac{{{f}_{0}}}{{{f}_{e}}}\left[ 1+\frac{{{f}_{e}}}{D} \right]$

3.In a telescope, if field and eye lenses are interchanged MP will change from ( $\left. {{f}_{0}}/{{f}_{e}} \right)$ to ( $\left. {{f}_{e}}/{{f}_{0}} \right)$, i.e., it will change from $\text{m}$ to $\left( 1/\text{m} \right)$, i.e., will become $\left( 1/{{\text{m}}^{2}} \right)$ times of its initial value.

4.As MP for normal setting as $\left( {{\text{f}}_{0}}/{{\text{f}}_{\text{e}}} \right.$ ), so to have large MP, ${{\text{f}}_{0}}$ must be as large as practically possible and ${{\text{f}}_{\text{e}}}$ small.

5.If an astronomical telescope, the convergent eye-piece is replaced by a divergent lens which is placed in such a way that rays from objective are directed towards its focus final image

Galilean telescope will be erect, enlarged and virtual. This telescope is also used to see distant terrestrial objects and is called Galilean telescope and for it $MP=\frac{{{f}_{0}}}{{{f}_{e}}}$ with $L={{f}_{0}}={{f}_{e}}$

In this telescope as intermediate image is outside the tube, the telescope cannot be used for making measurements.
